resolved.title: [Creole Petroleum Corp. well being drilled in the Tia Juana field on Lake Maracaibo, Venezuela, Feb. 1944]

decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1976, Venezuela. The government must decide how to nationalize the oil industry — through immediate, uncompensated seizure of foreign oil companies' assets (a dramatic break that could trigger international retaliation), or a negotiated, compensated transition that preserved diplomatic and business relationships but risked appearing weak to a public eager for full sovereignty over its own oil wealth. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: other Latin American nationalizations in this era had ranged from peaceful settlements to violent international disputes.Player choice: Seize foreign oil assets immediately without compensation, or negotiate a structured, compensated nationalization.Historical resolution: Venezuela pursued a negotiated, largely compensated nationalization, formally completed January 2, 1976, creating PDVSA — widely described by retrospectives as unusually quiet compared to other era nationalizations.

understand.economic_impact: Venezuela's economy became almost entirely dependent on oil revenue following the 1914 Zumaque-I discovery, and decades of institutional mismanagement following the 1976 nationalization eventually produced one of the most severe currency collapses in modern history, requiring three separate redenominations since 2008 Planet Banknote.
understand.political_impact: Naming the currency after Simón Bolívar tied Venezuela's national identity directly to its currency's fate — making the bolívar's collapse feel like a betrayal of the independence hero's legacy, not just an economic failure [TH Script Writing Bible voice profile]. The 1976 oil nationalization was a major assertion of national sovereignty over natural resources, following the same independence-era logic that named the currency Wikipedia: PDVSA.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Venezuela had one of the greatest natural advantages any country could ask for — more oil underground than almost anywhere else on Earth — and still ended up with one of history's worst currency collapses, forcing the government to cross zeros off its own money three separate times since 2008 just to keep numbers manageable. That gap between what a country has and what its people actually get to keep is the real lesson here: having valuable resources doesn't automatically mean people are protected from economic disaster, if the money and power around those resources aren't managed honestly. Simón's story matters because it's a warning about what happens when enormous wealth and weak accountability exist in the same place at the same time.
